coredata.dc:title: A new turn-off fluorescence probe based on graphene quantum dots for detection of Au(III) ion
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Amjadi, Mohammad
Shokri, Roghayeh
Hallaj, Tooba
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a new turn-off fluorescence probe based on the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was designed for detection and quantification of Au(III) ion. GQDs were prepared by two simple carbonization methods using glucose (g-GQDs) and citric acid (c-GQDs) as carbon sources. The effect of some metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of the prepared GQDs was studied. It was found that the fluorescence of both GQDs is significantly quenched by Au(III) ions but the sensitivity and analytical performances are different for two prepared GQDs. Using g-GQDs, a new analytical method was developed for the determination of Au(III) in the concentration range of 1.0–80μM, with a detection limit of 0.5μM. The developed method was applied to the determination of Au(III) in water and plasma samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescence quenching
Gold ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of glucose- and citric acid-derived GQDs
General procedure for determination of Au(III)
The quenching effect of some metal ions on the GQDs fluorescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of the prepared GQDs: (a) g-GQDs, (b) c-GQDs.
Fluorescence spectra for (a) g-GQDs and (b) c-GQDs, excited at 340 (a), 370 (b), 400 (c) and 430 (d) nm wavelengths.
The effect of some metal ions on the GQD fluorescence intensity. Conditions: (pH=6), GQD (1.0mL), buffer (8.0mM), time (10min), metal ion concentration (80μM).

The appearance and progressive increases of AuNP plasmon absorption band after addition of various concentrations of Au(III) to (a) g-GQDs, (b) c-GQDs. Conditions: (pH=6), GQD (1.0mL), buffer (8.0mM), time (10min).
Stern–Volmer plots for quenching of g-GQDs and c-GQDs by Au(III) ions at optimum conditions.

Optimization of the fluorescence quenching conditions: (a) Effect of pH value. Conditions: GQD (1.0mL), buffer (6.0mM), time (10min), Au(III) (10μM); (b) Effect of GQD solution volume. Conditions: buffer (8.0mM), other conditions are as in a; (c) Effect of time. GQD (1.0mL), other conditions are as in c.
The fluorescence emission spectra of g-GQDs (a) and c-GQDs (b) as a function of Au(III) concentration at optimum conditions (λex =400nm).
Analytical performance of the designed probes for determination of Au(III).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ΔF=F0 −F, F and F0 the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of Au(III), respectively, and C is concentration of Au(III) ions (μM).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the developed method for the determination Au(III) with some previously published methods.
Results for the determination of Au(III) in real samples.

head.ce:title: A new turn-off fluorescence probe based on graphene quantum dots for detection of Au(III) ion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a new turn-off fluorescence probe based on the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was designed for detection and quantification of Au(III) ion. GQDs were prepared by two simple carbonization methods using glucose (g-GQDs) and citric acid (c-GQDs) as carbon sources. The effect of some metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of the prepared GQDs was studied. It was found that the fluorescence of both GQDs is significantly quenched by Au(III) ions but the sensitivity and analytical performances are different for two prepared GQDs. Using g-GQDs, a new analytical method was developed for the determination of Au(III) in the concentration range of 1.0–80μM, with a detection limit of 0.5μM. The developed method was applied to the determination of Au(III) in water and plasma samples with satisfactory results.
Glucose-derived GQDs are selectively quenched by Au(III) ions, which form a basis for detection and quantification of Au(III).

ce:section.ce:para: In the past two decades, luminescent nanomaterials, such as semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [1], metal nanoclusters [2], dye-doped silica nanoparticles [3–5] and carbon nanomaterials [6,7] have been considered as alternatives to traditional organic fluorophores due to their unique physicochemical properties and promising applications in various fields. The new member of luminescent carbon nanomaterials, which has been discovered very recently, is graphene quantum dots (GQDs). They are graphene fragments with a size distribution below 100nm, which exhibit pronounced exciton confinement and quantum size effects. GQDs, unlike graphene that does not exhibit photoluminescence (PL), have non-zero band gap and show intense and stable PL. Moreover, in comparison to conventional semiconductor QDs, GQDs have superior properties such as ease of production, resistance to photobleaching, low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, higher surface areas, excellent solubility, chemical inertia, stable PL, better surface grafting, high electrical conductivity and high thermal conductivity [8–11]. These exceptional characteristics of GQDs make them promising in various fields such as in biological imaging and labeling [12], photovoltaic devices [13,14], catalysis [15], electrochemiluminescence [16] and chemiluminescence [17]. Recently, GQDs, synthesized from different methods, have been studied as a fluorescent sensing platform for the detection of ions [18–25], phenolic compounds [26–28], TNT [29], protein kinase [30], biothiols [31] and blood glucose [32]. To the best of our knowledge, the fluorescent sensing of Au(III) based on the GQDs have not yet been reported.

Gold is one of the important noble metals due to its wide application in industry and economic activity. For chemists, gold holds particular fascination in catalysis, surface and synthetic chemistry, and materials and theoretical investigations. Furthermore, gold ions can suppress inflammation in rheumatic joints. Gold-based drugs are effective for the treatment of various diseases such as arthritis, tuberculosis and cancer. Also, recently gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been extensively used in various fields. Despite the interesting chemical and medicinal properties of gold ions, soluble gold salts such as gold chloride are known to cause damage to the liver, kidneys, and the peripheral nervous system [33]. Therefore, the analysis of gold ions has appeared of great importance and various analytical methods such as fluorescence [33–37], spectrophotometry [33,38,39], atomic absorption spectrometry [40], inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry [41] and electrochemistry [42] have been reported for this purpose.

Recently, our research group has successfully prepared the fluorescent GQDs by a simple carbonization method using glucose as a carbon source. Herein, the applicability of the synthesized GQDs as fluorescence probes for the monitoring of metal ions was investigated. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of GQDs is quenched in the presence of Au(III) ions. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching was investigated. Moreover, Based on the mentioned quenching effect, a new florescence probe was developed for the monitoring of Au(III) in real water samples with satisfactory results.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu RF-540 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan) equipped with a 1cm quartz cell. UV/vis absorption spectra were obtained by a Cary-100 spectrophotometer (Varian, Sydney, Australia). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the GQDs were obtained using a Leo 906 transmission electron microscope (Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany).

All reagents were of analytical reagent grade. Doubly distilled deionized water (obtained from Ghazi Serum Co., Tabriz, Iran) was used throughout the experiment. Citric acid monohydrate, Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and Glucose were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Hydrogen tetra chloroaurate (III) was purchased from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, MA, USA). A stock standard solution of HAuCl4 (100μM) was prepared by diluting 100μL of solution of HAuCl4 (50mM) to 50mL with deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of glucose- and citric acid-derived GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Glucose-derived GQDs (g-GQDs) and citric acid-derived GQDs (c-GQDs) were synthesized by pyrolyzing glucose and citric acid, respectively, according to our previous works [17,43–45]. For synthesis of g-GQDs, 6.0g of glucose was put into a glass beaker and heated on a hot plate until the glucose powder was melted. After about 1.5min, the color of liquid was changed from colorless to orange. This color change implies the formation of GQDs. At this stage, the heating was stopped and 25mL of deionized water was added into the orange liquid with continuous and vigorous stirring. For synthesis of c-GQDs, 2.0g of citric acid was put into a beaker and heated to 200°C by a heating mantle until the citric acid changed to an orange liquid. Then, 100mL of 10mgmL−1 of NaOH solution was added into the orange liquid dropwise with continuous, vigorous stirring. Both prepared GQD solutions were stable for at least one month in the refrigerator at 4°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for determination of Au(III)
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 1.0mL of g-GQD solution and 200μL of 0.12M Britton–Robinson buffer (pH6.0) solution were added to a centrifuge tube. Then, an appropriate amount of Au(III) standard or sample solution was added to this tube and the final volume of the mixture was adjusted to 3.0mL with deionized water. After incubation for 10min at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity was recorded at 475nm with an excitation wavelength of 400nm.

Alternatively, 1.0mL of c-GQD solutions and 1.5mL of 0.12M acetate buffer solution (pH5.0) were added to a centrifuge tube. Then, an appropriate amount of Au(III) standard or sample solution was added to the tube and the final volume was adjusted to 4.0mL with deionized water. After incubation for 10min at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity was recorded at 470nm with an excitation wavelength of 400nm.

Human plasma samples were obtained from Blood Transfusion Center (Tabriz, Iran). A 500μL of aliquot of plasma was placed into a centrifuge tube and spiked by adding appropriate volumes of Au(III) standard solution. 2.0mL of 0.1M Ba(OH)2 and 1.8mL of 0.1M ZnSO4 were added into the tube for precipitation of proteins. Then the solution was centrifuged at 14,000rpm for 5min. The supernatant solution was transferred into a 5.0mL volumetric flask and diluted to the mark with water. Appropriate portion of this solution was analyzed according to the general procedure.
No pretreatment was necessary for water samples.

In this work, a simple carbonization method was used for synthesis of GQDs from glucose and citric acid as carbon sources. TEM images of synthesized GQDs are shown (Fig. 1 ). They indicate that both GQDs are nearly monodisperse nanoparticles. The size distribution of GQDs is also shown in Fig. 1, which indicates the average sizes of ~22 and ~8nm for g-GQDs and c-GQDs, respectively. More characterization of these nanoparticles was given in Electronic Supplementary Materials (ESM).

The fluorescence behavior of these GQDs was investigated. For both GQDs, the maximum emission of ∼470nm was obtained with an excitation wavelength of 400nm. However, as shown in Fig. 2 , g-GQDs exhibited an excitation-dependent luminescent behavior, while the fluorescence of c-GQDs showed no excitation-dependent feature. Although, the origin of GQD fluorescence is a matter of current debate, it is generally believed that the quantum confinement and edge effects play major roles in this phenomenon [8,11]. The red shift in emission wavelength with increasing excitation wavelength in g-GQDs is attributed to a distribution of the different surface state emissive traps, while the excitation wavelength-independence of c-GQDs can be attributed to the uniformity both in the size and the surface state of those sp2 clusters responsible for fluorescence [44,45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The quenching effect of some metal ions on the GQDs fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para:
Fluorescence quenching data are generally presented as Stern–Volmer plots i.e. the plot F0/F versus quencher concentration:
(1)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
S V
Q
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of fluorophore in the absence and presence of quencher Q, respectively, KSV is the Stern–Volmer constant (M−1) and [Q] is the concentration of the quencher. In the case of static quenching, KSV corresponds to the binding constant of ground-state fluorophore–quencher complex [49]. According to Stern–Volmer equation it is expected that this plot shows a linear behavior for a pure dynamic and static quenching process. Stern–Volmer plots for the fluorescence quenching of GQDs by Au(III) are shown in Fig. 5 . As can be seen, both plots are linear with the slopes of 6.6×103 and 2.2×104 M−1 for g-GQDs and c-GQDs, respectively. We performed this experiment at three different temperatures (25, 35 and 45°C) but the slopes did not change significantly. This indicates that the quenching is probably static in nature.
In order to obtain the maximum sensitivity for the quenching system, the effects of several variables on the fluorescence quenching efficiency were investigated.

The effect of pH was studied in the range of 4 to 9 using appropriate buffer solutions (Fig. 6a). The highest fluorescence quenching efficiency (F−F0) was obtained at pH6.0 and 5.0 for g-GQDs and c-GQDs, respectively. At the acidic pH, with increasing concentration of H+, the aggregation of GQDs may occur due to the formation of hydrogen bond between oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of GQDs. As a result, the fluorescence quenching efficiency decreased at lower pH values. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching efficiency diminished at higher pH values probably because of the hydrolysis of Au(III). The effect of buffer concentration was also investigated. According to the results, the concentration of 8 and 45mM was selected to obtain the highest response for g-GQDs and c-GQDs, respectively.

The effect of the amount of GQD solutions on the quenching efficiency was also studied in the range of 300–1500μL of stock solution. As shown in Fig. 6b, the quenching efficiency increased by increasing GQD volume up to 1000μL. At the higher concentration, self quenching and/or aggregation of GQDs may be occurred. Therefore, this amount was selected as the optimum value for experiments.

Finally, the variation in fluorescence quenching intensity over time after addition of Au(III) was studied (Fig. 6c). Based on the obtained results, all fluorescence measurements were performed 10min after the addition of the analyte.

A fluorescence probe was designed for detection of Au(III) ions based on their quenching effect on both GQDs. The fluorescence spectra of g-GQDs and c-GQDs with varying concentrations of Au(III) ions are shown in Fig. 7 . It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of both GQDs decreases with increasing concentration of Au(III) ions, indicating that interactions between these ions and GQDs occur in the system. Under the optimum conditions described above, the analytical performance of the developed probes for the determination of Au(III) was obtained (Table 1 ). The results indicate that the designed fluorescence probe based on g-GQD has much wider linear range and better detection limit than that of designed based on c-GQDs, though the sensitivity of the latter is higher than the former. Therefore, we applied the probe based on g-GQD for the analysis of real samples. Analytical performance of the developed sensor was compared with that of some other reported fluorescent probes in Table 2 . The linear range of our method is comparable with the reported methods but, it is much simpler and more rapid. Especially compared with graphene oxide-poly(vinyl alcohol) hybrid [37] the preparation of our GQDs is very simple and does not need any harsh condition.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of the developed method, the effects of some common inorganic ions on the determination of 5.0μM of Au(III) were investigated. The tolerable concentration ratios for interferences in relative error of <5% were over 1500 for Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Zn2+, As(V), NO3 −, Cl−, SO4 2−, and PO4 3−, 400 for Hg2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Bi3+, and Sn2+, 100 for Al3+, 50 for Cr(VI) and 25 for Pt(IV). These results show that the developed probe is quite selective for Au(III) and can be applied for interference-free determination of this ion in real samples.

In order to evaluate the capability of the designed probe in real sample analysis, it was applied to the determination of Au(III) ions in spiked water as well as human plasma samples. As mentioned in the Introduction section, some gold compounds are used in treatment of rheumatoid arthritis, so monitoring of Au(III) in plasma of patients under such treatments is important to suppress the side effects which are caused by gold concentrations above about 10μM [50]. The obtained results were summarized in Table 3 . Statistical analysis of these results using Student t-test showed that there are no significant differences between added and found values.

In this work, we introduced a new fluorescent probe for selective detection of Au(III) ions based on their quenching effect on the fluorescence of GQDs. The preparation procedure for GQDs is very simple, rapid and safe. The possible mechanism of fluorescence quenching was investigated. The quenching mechanism may be attributed to the energy and/or electron transfer from GQDs to AuNPs and Au(III) ions, respectively. We found that, the response of GQDs due to quenching by Au(III) ions was concentration-dependent and so based on this phenomenon, a simple and sensitive fluorescent probe was designed for the determination of Au(III) ions with good linearity, high selectivity and precision, which can be applied to the analysis of real samples.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2015.09.037.
